coredata.dc:title: Green, facial zinc doped hydrothermal synthesis of cinnamon derived fluorescent carbon dots (Zn-Cn-CDs) for highly selective and sensitive Cr6+ and Mn7+ metal ion sensing application
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Kolekar, Akanksha G.
Nille, Omkar S.
Koparde, Sneha V.
Patil, Akshay S.
Waghmare, Ravindra D.
Sohn, Daewon
Anbhule, Prashant V.
Kolekar, Govind B.
Gokavi, Gavisiddappa S.
More, Vishalkumar R.
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots have demonstrated a great potential as luminescent nanoparticles in energy, drug delivery, sensors, and various biomedical applications as well as environmental pollutants and water analysis. Although, such nanoparticles appear to exhibit low toxicity compared to other semiconductor and metal based luminescent nanomaterials. Today, we know that toxicity of carbon dots (CDs) strongly depends on the protocol of fabrication. The various dopants or heteroatoms have been used to enhance the optical and physicochemical properties. In this work, zinc doped aqueous fluorescent Zn-Cn-CDs have been synthesized from cinnamon by hydrothermal synthesis method. The synthesized Zn-Cn-CDs were confirmed for their physicochemical properties by using various characterization techniques viz. UV–Vis. and spectrofluorometer for optical properties, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) and XRD, as well as TEM and XPS, was done for morphological and chemical analysis. The successfully synthesized Zn-Cn-CDs showed outstanding optical performance for metal ion sensing applications. The developed heteroatom doped Zn-Cn-CDs as a fluorescent probe exhibited higher selectivity and sensitivity for Cr6+ and Mn7+ metal ions. The obtained results showed a better linear range with excellent limit of detection (LOD) 3.97 µg/mL and 2.05 µg/mL for Cr6+ and Mn7+ metal ions respectively. The low cost, simple and highly fluorescent probe can be effectively applicable for development of environmental pollutants sensing purposes.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Zinc doped cinnamon carbon dots (Zn-Cn-CDs)
Hydrothermal
Heteroatom Doping
Fluorescent probe
Metal ion sensor
Environmental remediation

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Hydrothermal synthesis of zinc doped cinnamon carbon dots (Zn-Cn-CDs)
Characterization of zinc doped cinnamon carbon dots (Zn-Cn-CDs)
Selectivity and sensing study of Cr6+ and Mn7+ by Zn-Cn-CDs
Detection of Cr6+ and Mn7+ ions

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Department of Chemistry, Aligarh Muslim University

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We are very much thankful to the Department of Chemistry, Shivaji University, Kolhapur, Department of Chemistry, The New College, Kolhapur for the lab facilities as well as characterizations. As well as Department of Chemistry, Hanyang University, Seoul, South Korea for providing characterization facilities, Daewon Sohn and Akshay Patil thanks to the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) 2020R1A6A1A06046728 and 2022R1A2C1010580.

We are very much thankful to the Department of Chemistry, Shivaji University, Kolhapur, Department of Chemistry, The New College, Kolhapur for the lab facilities as well as characterizations. As well as Department of Chemistry, Hanyang University, Seoul, South Korea for providing characterization facilities, Daewon Sohn and Akshay Patil thanks to the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) 2020R1A6A1A06046728 and 2022R1A2C1010580.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The schematic for the hydrothermal synthesis of zinc doped cinnamon carbon dots (Zn-Cn-CDs).
(a) UV–Visible and, (b) Fluorescence emission peaks of Zn-Cn-CDs at different excitations.
(a) FT-IR and, (b) XRD spectrum of zinc doped cinnamon carbon dots (Zn-Cn-CDs).
The XPS spectra of Zn-Cn-CDs, a) survey spectra, and high-resolution b) C1s, c) O1s, d) Zn2p spectra.
The TEM image of Zn-Cn-CDs, b) the particle size distribution graph and c) showing the SAED pattern.

The selectivity of fluorescent zinc doped cinnamon carbon dots (Zn-Cn-CDs) towards Cr6+ and Mn7+ metal ions and b) showing the F/F0 bar graph for selectivity of different metal ions, c) UV–Vis. absorption spectra Zn-Cn-CDs with different metal ions.
The effect of concentration of (a) Cr6+ ions on fluorescence of Zn-Cn-CDs, b) showing the Stern-Volmer plot for quenching of Cr6+ ions.
The effect of concentration of (a) Mn7+ ions on fluorescence of Zn-Cn-CDs, b) showing the Stern-Volmer plot for quenching of Mn7+ ions.
Effect of several coexisting metal ions in presence and absence of a) Cr6+, b) Mn7+ ions and c) effect of time on quenching of Cr6+ and Mn7+ ions.
The fluorescence decay profile of Zn-Cn-CDs in presence and absence of a) Cr6+ and b) Mn7+, c) UV–Vis. absorption spectra of Zn-Cn-CDs in presence and absence of Cr6+ and Mn7+.
Different carbon dots based sensors for Cr6+ and Mn7+ detection.
The real water sample analysis of Zn-Cn-CDs for detection of Cr6+ and Mn7+ ions.

entry.bold: Amount of Standard Cr6+, Mn7+ spiked (µg/mL)
Recovery of Cr6+ and Mn7+ added (%) (n = 3)

head.ce:title: Green, facial zinc doped hydrothermal synthesis of cinnamon derived fluorescent carbon dots (Zn-Cn-CDs) for highly selective and sensitive Cr6+ and Mn7+ metal ion sensing application

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Fluorescence Spectroscopy Research Laboratory, Department of Chemistry, Shivaji University, Kolhapur, Maharashtra, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Fluorescence Spectroscopy Research Laboratory, Department of Chemistry, Shivaji University, Kolhapur, Maharashtra, India

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry and Research Institute for Convergence of Basic Science, Hanyang University, Seoul Campus, Seoul, South Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry and Research Institute for Convergence of Basic Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry and Research Institute for Convergence of Basic Science, Hanyang University, Seoul Campus, Seoul, South Korea

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Shivaji University, Kolhapur, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Shivaji University, Kolhapur, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, The New College, Kolhapur, MS, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, The New College, Kolhapur, MS, India

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Department of Chemistry, The New College, Kolhapur, MS, India.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots have demonstrated a great potential as luminescent nanoparticles in energy, drug delivery, sensors, and various biomedical applications as well as environmental pollutants and water analysis. Although, such nanoparticles appear to exhibit low toxicity compared to other semiconductor and metal based luminescent nanomaterials. Today, we know that toxicity of carbon dots (CDs) strongly depends on the protocol of fabrication. The various dopants or heteroatoms have been used to enhance the optical and physicochemical properties. In this work, zinc doped aqueous fluorescent Zn-Cn-CDs have been synthesized from cinnamon by hydrothermal synthesis method. The synthesized Zn-Cn-CDs were confirmed for their physicochemical properties by using various characterization techniques viz. UV–Vis. and spectrofluorometer for optical properties, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) and XRD, as well as TEM and XPS, was done for morphological and chemical analysis. The successfully synthesized Zn-Cn-CDs showed outstanding optical performance for metal ion sensing applications. The developed heteroatom doped Zn-Cn-CDs as a fluorescent probe exhibited higher selectivity and sensitivity for Cr6+ and Mn7+ metal ions. The obtained results showed a better linear range with excellent limit of detection (LOD) 3.97 µg/mL and 2.05 µg/mL for Cr6+ and Mn7+ metal ions respectively. The low cost, simple and highly fluorescent probe can be effectively applicable for development of environmental pollutants sensing purposes.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The rapidly increasing industrialization and urbanization have laid to the tremendous destruction of environmental resources. The processes involved in development of newer materials including metallurgy, dying, printing, leather, pharmaceutical, agrochemicals, and mining etc. are generating huge amount of wastes and which contaminating the water bodies without pretreatments [1–3]. Generally, the toxic and heavy metal ions is the main source of contaminants in environmental resources. The commonly found chromium (Cr) and manganese (Mn) are extensively used in various abovementioned processes. In environment, generally the chromium was found in Cr6+, Cr3+ as well as manganese in Mn7+ oxidation states which were used as an essential nutrient source in biological processes, as a antiseptic and disinfectant materials, and many more industrial processes [4,5]. The contamination and long exposure to these metals can lead to the serious health issues viz. diarrhea, vomiting, skin and respiratory infections, renal damage, cytotoxicity and severely damages the central nervous system, cancer, etc [6]. Henceforth, there is an urgent need to develop simple, cost effective as well as sensitive and selective method for detection of Cr6+ and Mn7+ pollutants.

Nanotechnology is the design, production, characterization, uses of structures, devices and systems by controlling size and shape at the nanometre scale between 1 and 100 nm [7,8]. Nanotechnology is part of the next wave of innovation in science and engineering that will transform many sectors including aerospace, energy, information technology, medicine, national defence and transportation [9–11]. The Carbon-based nanomaterials are special class of nanomaterials including carbon nanotubes, fullerenes, graphene and its derivatives fluorescent carbon dots and graphene oxide [12,13]. Fluorescent carbon dots are a type of novel carbon-based nanomaterial (generally the diameter < 10 nm) with a photoluminescent behaviour since their discovery in 2004 the preparation of fluorescent carbon dots and their applications have been continually expanded with advanced optical and physico-chemical properties over other fluorescent nanomaterials [14–16]. The carbon dots have various advantages such as desirable size and excitation wavelength dependent photoluminescence, photostability, easy surface fictionalization, low toxicity good biocompatibility [17–19]. Carbon dots (CDs) have nearly spherical shapes having an amorphous and crystalline core with predominant sp2 carbon and oxidized carbon surface with carboxyl, hydroxyl groups etc. Recently, CDs have evolved tremendous interests in researchers as a potential candidate to substitute toxic metal-based Quantum dots (QDs), thus have great potential in various applications [20–22].

The variety of synthetic approaches for CDs can be categorized into two main routes a) top down, b) bottom up methods including laser ablation, Arc discharge, electrochemical approach, microwave irradiation, chemical oxidation, carbonization, direct pyrolysis and hydrothermal reaction [23–25]. Among all these methods, the CDs synthesize via hydrothermal treatment is the most popular approach because the synthesis process is simple clean and economical [15,24]. Owing to its low cost and ease of implementation hydrothermal synthesis is the most common and green process for CDs preparation. This thermal mediated approach requires pressurized auto cleve vessel’s reaction temperature ranging from 120 0C to 240 0C and the reaction times of 3–24 hrs, in a typical synthesis [25,26]. Recently, the heteroatom doped CDs are potentially attracted the researchers and scientists around the globe [27]. The heteroatom doping into carbon core have triggered the tunable optical, chemical and physical properties with enhanced quantum yield of fluorescent carbon dots [17]. In addition to that, the doped metal ions/ heteroatoms into CDs can effectively impart the conducting, antimicrobial properties with multifaceted applications [18,19]. So far, the research group of Zhengjun Shi have synthesized N-CDs for detection of MnO4 - ions with 0.12 µM LOD[28]. Also, S. B. Wakshe from our research group have developed fluorescent organic nanosheets for recognition of Cr6+ and Mn7+ metal ions [6]. Also, Sumit Kumar Sonkar and co-workers have reported the CZnO-Dots for the photoreduction of Cr6+ to Cr3+ under sunlight induced conditions [29].

The diverse optical, chemical, fluorescence, biocompatible, chemical inertness, and such excellent properties of fluorescent carbon dots makes it multifaceted applicative. In present work, the kitchen derived cinnamon was used as a precursor material of carbon and zinc acetate was used as a dopant for synthesis of Zn-Cn-CDs. The simple, cost effective and reliable hydrothermal method was employed for synthesis of Zn-Cn-CDs. The successfully synthesized Zn-Cn-CDs was confirmed through various characterization and analytical techniques and effectively used for highly selective metal ion detection applications.

All chemical reagents were of analytical reagent grade and used directly as received without further purification. All aqueous solutions of metal ions were prepared by using respective metal salts of analytical grade with distilled water (D.W.). The cinnamon as a kitchen material was used as a precursor material for synthesis of CDs. The zinc acetate (Zn(CH3COO)2.(H2O)2, M.W.: 219.50 g/mol, Purity: 99.00 %) was used as a heteroatom for doping into CDs. The metal ions used for detection study were NiCl2, CoCl2·6H2O, BaCl2, CaCl2·2H2O, NaCl, ZnCl2, HgCl2, SnCl2·2H2O, MgCl2·6H2O, KMnO4, K2Cr2O7, FeCl3.6 H2O, FeSO4·7H2O CuSO4·2H2O, etc. of analytical grade and purchased from Sisco Research Laboratory (SRL, PVT, LTD.). Double distilled water is used throughout the whole experiment.

The absorption spectrum was acquired at room temperature by using Specord 210 plus, Analytikjena UV–Vis. spectrophotometer with the use of 1 cm quartz cuvette. The fluorescence measurements of Zn-Cn-CDs and solutions was made with PC based spectrofluorometer (Model- Jasco FP 8300, Japan). The fluorescence decay study was conducted by using time resolved single photon count study (Horiba, DeltaFlex). The surface functional groups present on Zn-Cn-CDs were studied by using Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy (Bruker- α, Germany) as well as the crystallinity study of Zn-Cn-CDs was carried out by using X-ray diffraction (XRD) study using CuKα radiations (Bruker D8 Advance, Germany). The chemical composition of Zn-Cn-CDs was analyzed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), (Kratos Analytical, AXIS Supra). The particle size and morphology of Zn-Cn-CDs was examined using transmission electron microscopy (TEM), (FEI Tecnai G2, F30).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Hydrothermal synthesis of zinc doped cinnamon carbon dots (Zn-Cn-CDs)
ce:section.ce:para: In this process, we have used the kitchen material cinnamon and doping of zinc (Zinc acetate) as a precursor materials for synthesis of zinc doped carbon dots (Zn-Cn-CDs). Cinnamon the eternal tree of tropical medicine belongs to the Lauraceae family and one of the most important daily used spices by people all over the word. Cinnamon primarily contains vital essential oil and other derivatives such as cinnamaldehyde, cinnamic acid and cinnamate [30]. The Fig. 1 showing the hydrothermal synthesis of zinc doped cinnamon carbon dots (Zn-Cn-CDs).

Here, we have used cinnamon and zinc (zinc acetate) for doping as a precursor material for the synthesis of Zn-Cn-CDs. 0.1 gm of finely crushed powder of cinnamon was weighed and 0.1 gm of zinc acetate was added in 10 mL of D.W. and homogenized on magnetic stirrer. This reaction mixture was transferred into Teflon (PTFE) lined autoclave and the temperature was set to be 180 0C for 16 hrs. After completion of the reaction the hydrothermal autoclave was allowed to cool down to room temperature, and the solution was filtrated using Whatman filter paper No. 41 afterword’s, solution diluted up to 50 mL with distilled water. The synthesized zinc doped cinnamon carbon dots (Zn-Cn-CDs) were further purified by centrifugation at 8000 rpm for 20 min and then passed with 0.22 µm syringe filter. Finally, the successfully synthesized zinc doped cinnamon carbon dots (Zn-Cn-CDs) were prepared in stock and stored in refrigerator for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of zinc doped cinnamon carbon dots (Zn-Cn-CDs)
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesized Zn-Cn-CDs are ascertained by wide range of spectroscopic, surface and microscopic techniques. Among them, UV–Vis., fluorescence, XRD, FTIR, XPS, and TEM analysis were employed for confirmation of physicochemical properties of Zn-Cn-CDs as described below.

The UV–Visible spectroscopy is useful characterization technique utilized to know the absorption band of the synthesized nanomaterials as a function of the wavelength. The absorption of light either from UV (∼200–400 nm) or visible (∼400–800 nm) region results in the movement of electrons from ground state to higher energy exited state and gives distinct spectrum. The energy (wavelength) of absorbed light is corresponds to the difference in energy between ground and exited state. Thus, reduction of nanomaterial size particle shifts the wavelength of absorption to blue region. The absorption peak position is useful to elucidate transitions in between π-π*, n-π*, and σ-σ* level and ultimately electronic structures of materials [31].

From the literature survey, it was observed that the absorption of CDs is depending on the type of synthesis method, selection of precursor, and other experimental conditions [32]. Generally, CDs have some graphite carbon core and have different carboxyl, hydroxyl, and amine functional groups on their surfaces. Therefore, most of the absorption peaks are due to their electronic transitions.

The optical properties of Zn-Cn-CDs were studied by UV–Vis. and fluorescence spectroscopy. The absorption spectrum showed that the synthesized material showed characteristic peaks at 260 nm and a small hump at 320 nm. The absorption peak at 260 nm could be associated with the π-π* transition of the C = C core whereas, the peak at 320 nm correlates to the n-π* transitions of the (-C = O) carbonyl group present on the surface of Zn-Cn-CDs. Fig. 2 (a) represents the UV–Vis. spectra of Zn-Cn-CDs [31].

The fluorescence emission spectra of Zn-Cn-CDs at different excitation wavelengths is shown in Fig. 2 b. The synthesized Zn-Cn-CDs have bright fluorescence emission properties. However, the origin of fluorescence of CDs is still under exploration, it is assumed that it may be due to the surface trap states, surface defects, etc. Moreover, the fluorescence properties of Zn-Cn-CDs have been studied by changing the excitation wavelength from 290 to 380 nm by 10 nm gap. The Zn-Cn-CDs exhibited maximum fluorescence emission at λem = 450 nm when excited at λex = 330 nm. It was noticed that increasing excitation wavelength was accompanied by a gradual red shift to emission peaks with variable emission intensity. The results notice that, the Zn-Cn-CDs exhibited excitation dependent emission properties.

FTIR spectroscopy is one of the most important and necessary characterization tools to evaluate the presence of different functional groups on the surface of CDs. The presence of C-N bond, Amino acid, Amide, N–H bond, ketone, aldehyde, alcohol, halogens, etc. can be determined using IR spectroscopy, which is used to reveal the complex formation mechanism between CDs and analytes. The broad peak at 3350 cm−1 is due to the presence of hydroxyl (–OH) functionality, and the peak at 2215 cm−1 is due to C≡C stretching vibrations (Fig. 3 a). The prominent peak at 1623 cm−1 can be prescribed due to C = C stretching. The small peaks at 1315 cm−1 and 1110 cm−1 are due to C-O stretching [33]. The peak at 660 cm−1 is due to the presence of Zn-O stretching vibration of doped Zn metal into CDs [34]. It can be concluded from the FT-IR spectra that, Zn-Cn-CDs are rich in carboxyl, and hydroxyl functionalities and successfully doped with Zn metal into carbon core, which can help to interact with various analytes.

The amorphous and graphitic nature of Zn-Cn-CDs can be distinguished with the assistance of X-Ray diffraction study. The Fig. 3b shows the XRD spectra of Zn-Cn-CDs. The XRD pattern of Zn-Cn-CDs displayed a broad peak centered at 20° − 30° owing to the highly disordered structure of Zn-Cn-CDs. The broad peak centered at 23.5° can be prescribed for the (002) plane showing the amorphous nature of Zn-Cn-CDs [34,35]. The shift in the peak could be due the disordered sp2 layers with increased surface defects due to impregnation of Zn into carbon core [34].

The elemental composition of Zn-Cn-CDs was evaluated by using XPS analysis as shown in Fig. 4 . The XPS survey spectrum of Zn-Cn-CDs shows presence of peaks at 284.5 eV and 532.5 eV indicates the presence of C1s and O1s as well as peaks at 89 eV (Zn3p), 198.5 eV (Zn2s), 1021.5 eV (Zn2p3/2) and 1045 eV (Zn2p1/2) confirms the presence of Zn metal in carbon core [34,36]. The appearance of characteristic peaks of Zn in XPS spectra confirms the successful doping of Zn as a heteroatom into carbon core.

The morphology, shape and particle size of Zn-Cn-CDs was examined using TEM analysis. The Fig. 5 a suggests that the synthesized Zn-Cn-CDs having quasi spherical shape with uniform distribution. Along with that, the high-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) inset image of Zn-Cn-CDs indicating the interplanar spacing of d = 0.32 nm. The Fig. 5b showing the particle size distribution graph in the range of 3–7 nm having average particle size of 5 nm. The selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern of Zn-Cn-CDs having faded circular diffraction patterns proving the amorphous nature of CDs due to distorted graphitic sheets (Fig. 5c).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and sensing study of Cr6+ and Mn7+ by Zn-Cn-CDs
ce:section.ce:para:
The different metal ions such as Ni2+, Co2+, Ba2+, Ca2+, Na+, Zn2+, Hg2+, Sn2+, Mg2+, Mn7+, Cr6+, Cu2+, Fe2+ and Fe3+ were added into Zn-Cn-CDs, and the solution was analyzed. The results obtained indicates that only Cr6+ and Mn7+ show the decrease in fluorescence intensity due to quenching of fluorescence of Zn-Cn-CDs. The fluorescence sensor based on Zn-Cn-CDs shows a higher selectivity for Cr6+ and Mn7+, detection over the other metal ions. Hence, these metals ions can be used for further study (Figs. 7, 8
). The Fig. 6b indicating the bar graph of F/F0 for different metal ions, out of which the Cr6+, and Mn7+ metal ions depicting the diminished bar graphs showing the developed fluorescent probe having selectivity for specific metal ions. Also, the Fig. 6c showing the absorption spectra of Zn-Cn-CDs in presence of different metal ions. The results confirm the Zn-Cn-CDs in presence of Cr6+ and Mn7+ selectively showing deviation in absorption peaks than rest of metal ions. The observed results confirm that there was complex formation takes place between Zn-Cn-CDs and Cr6+ and Mn7+. In addition, the quantum yield of Zn-Cn-CDs was investigated in presence and absence of Cr6+ and Mn7+ (Fig. S1). The results suggest that, the quantum yield (QY) of Zn-Cn-CDs was found to be 7.47 %. While, the QY of Zn-Cn-CDs remains constant in presence of other metals in the range of 5.76 % to 7.46 %, whereas in case of Cr6+ and Mn7+ the QY was significantly dropped to 3.80 % and 2.41 % respectively. The bar graph in Fig. S1 shows diminished QY for Cr6+ and Mn7+ due to significant quenching of fluorescence intensity of Zn-Cn-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Cr6+ and Mn7+ ions
ce:section.ce:para:
For the study of linearity, we took different concentrations of both metal ions. These metal ions added into Zn-Cn-CDs solution. Then solutions were kept for 5 min and analyzed using spectrofluorometer. The results obtained indicated that the increasing concentration of both metal ions gradually decreases the fluorescence intensity of Zn-Cn-CDs i.e., shows quenching. This quenching of fluorescence was studied by the Stern-Volmer’s equation (Eq. (1)).
(1)
F 0
F
= 1 +
K
sv
[ Q ]
Where, F0 and F are the fluorescence intensities of Zn-Cn-CDs in the presence and absence of different metal ions. The Q is the concentration of quencher and Ksv is the Stern-Volmer quenching constant.

The Fig. 7(a, b) and Fig. 8(a, b) displayed gradual decrease in the fluorescence intensity of Zn-Cn-CDs upon increasing concentration of Cr6+ and Mn7+ ions in the range of 0 to 20 µg/mL respectively. The data obtained from Stern-Volmer plots exhibited good linear range with correlation coefficient (R2) values of 0.9887 and 0.9956 having limit of detection of 3.97 µg/mL and 2.05 µg/mL for Cr6+ and Mn7+ metal ions respectively. The Table 1 showing the comparison between Zn-Cn-CDs and previous reports for Cr6+ and Mn7+ ion sensing.

The selectivity and specificity for detection of metal ions are essential parameters. To explore the selectivity of Zn-Cn-CDs for Cr6+ and Mn7+ ions, the interference study of other competing metals was studied (Fig. 9 a, b). In present study, the aqueous solution of Zn-Cn-CDs was added with Cr6+ and Mn7+ ions (10 µg/mL) and all other competing ions were added separately and fluorescence spectra was recorded. The bar graph of change in fluorescence intensity (ΔF/F0) where, ΔF = F0 – F, and F0 are the fluorescence intensities of Zn-Cn-CDs in presence and absence of other coexisting metal ions. The results suggest that, there was no significant change or little change in fluorescence intensity was recorded for Zn-Cn-CDs with Cr6+ and Mn7+ ions in presence of other metal ions, while Zn-Cn-CDs shows deviation only for Cr6+ and Mn7+ ions respectively. The results conclude that, the other competing metal ions does not interfere with selectivity of Zn-Cn-CDs for Cr6+ and Mn7+ ions.

The rapidness and detection ability of Zn-Cn-CDs for Cr6+ and Mn7+ ions over a time was studied up to 30 min. The aqueous solution of Zn-Cn-CDs was added with Cr6+ and Mn7+ ions separately and quenching response was recorded (Fig. 9c). The results shown that within 2 min the maximum quenching of Zn-Cn-CDs takes place and further nearly remains constant up to 30 min. This proves that, the developed Zn-Cn-CDs highly selective and rapid fluorescent probe for detection of Cr6+ and Mn7+ ions.

The fluorescence sensing mechanism of Zn-Cn-CDs for detection of Cr6+ and Mn7+ can be well illustrated using fluorescence decay profile, i.e., time correlated single photon count study (TCSPC). The quenching of analyte by using fluorophore having different ground state and/or excited state interactions. The sensing mechanism mainly consisting of different interactions viz. ground state complex formation (static quenching), excited state interaction (dynamic quenching), Föster resonance energy transfer (FRET) and photoinduced electron transfer (PET) mechanisms [6]. In present investigation, the fluorescence decay profile of Zn-Cn-CDs in absence and presence of Cr6+ and Mn7+ was recorded (Fig. 10 a, b). The results suggest that, as we increased the concentration of Cr6+ there was no any change in the decay profile of Zn-Cn-CDs which clearly confirming the formation of ground state complex following the static type of quenching. While, in case of Mn7+ the increasing concentration decreased the fluorescence lifetime gradually, which confirming the excited state interaction between Mn7+ and Zn-Cn-CDs, indicating the dynamic type of quenching. In addition, the quenching mechanism can be confirmed using UV–Vis. absorption study. The Fig. 10c showing the absorption spectra of Zn-Cn-CDs in presence and absence of Cr6+ and Mn7+ ions. The results indicated that, the interaction between Zn-Cn-CDs and Mn7+ ions completely vanish the peak and change in peak wavelength in presence of Cr6+ and Mn7+ ions clearly confirming the interaction between CDs and metal ions and complex formation takes place.

The practical applicability and feasibility of the developed Zn-Cn-CDs as a fluorescent probe was applied for real water sample analysis. In present study, the water samples from nearby water sources i.e., Rajaram lake water and Shivaji University, lake water was collected. First of all, the collected water sample was filtered through Whatman’s filter paper No. 41 to remove residual contents and again boiled for 10 min to remove dissolved gases. The different known concentrations of Cr6+ and Mn7+ were spiked into water samples and recorded the fluorescence and results are summarized in Table 2 . The developed Zn-Cn-CDs as a fluorescent probe having recovery in the range of 95 to 98 % with very good relative standard deviation (RSD). The results indicate that the Zn-Cn-CDs having applications for real life water sample analysis.

In summary, an efficient, simple and economical approach has been utilized to synthesize Zn-Cn-CDs excellently. The hydrothermal route (bottom-up approach) was utilized for the zinc doped synthesis of Zn-Cn-CDs at 180 0C for 16 hrs. The as synthesized Zn-Cn-CDs exhibited ideal fluorescent property showing bright blue coloured fluorescence under UV light. The as synthesized Zn-Cn-CDs have excitation dependent behavior showing the highest emission intensity (446 nm) at an excitation wavelength of 330 nm. It was further used as highly selective and sensitive fluorescent probe for the determination of Cr6+ and Mn7+ by quenching the fluorescence of Zn-Cn-CDs. Additionally, this sensing platform exhibited high selectivity in solution. The developed Zn-Cn-CDs fluorescent probe exhibited good linear correlation having limit of detection of 3.97 µg/mL and 2.05 µg/mL for Cr6+ and Mn7+ metal ions respectively. This efficient strategy was simple, facile and low cost, which may find potential application in biomedical and pharmaceutical samples.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We are very much thankful to the Department of Chemistry, Shivaji University, Kolhapur, Department of Chemistry, The New College, Kolhapur for the lab facilities as well as characterizations. As well as Department of Chemistry, Hanyang University, Seoul, South Korea for providing characterization facilities, Daewon Sohn and Akshay Patil thanks to the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) 2020R1A6A1A06046728 and 2022R1A2C1010580.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2023.123413.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
